title: Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the NERC CIP programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with NERC CIP control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.34: Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.34' running on index=wineventlog EventCode 4624/4625, bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper NERC CIP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the NERC CIP control objective.

dataSources: `index=wineventlog` `EventCode` 4624/4625, `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode IN (4624,4625) earliest=-24h
| lookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv ComputerName AS host OUTPUT bes_system
| where isnotnull(bes_system)
| eval outcome=if(EventCode==4625,"fail","success")
| stats count by Account_Name, ComputerName, outcome, bes_system
| sort - count
description: We summarize authentication successes and failures on BES Cyber Assets to support account lockout and misuse investigations.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure 4625 auditing enabled. (2) Add threshold alerts for failures. (3) Correlate with password spray use cases. (4) Retain per entity log policy. Indexes required: `index=wineventlog`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`; Lookups: `bes_cyber_asset_inventory`, `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv`.
visualization: Stacked bar (success vs fail), Table (top accounts), Single value (failure rate). Key fields: `Account_Name`, `ComputerName`, `bes_system`, `outcome`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Planned generation outage maintenance** — Scheduled maintenance outages on BES Cyber Systems require physical and electronic access that triggers CIP access monitoring alerts; these are pre-approved via the outage management system and documented in the maintenance plan.

2. **NERC compliance audit field activities** — During triennial CIP compliance audits, Regional Entity auditors perform physical walkthroughs, system sampling, and configuration reviews that generate access and configuration query events.

3. **Relay and protection system testing** — Periodic relay testing, protection coordination studies, and SCADA system communication tests produce legitimate BES operational signals that are scheduled and documented in the maintenance programme.

4. **Vendor remote access for approved patches** — ICS/SCADA vendors granted time-limited interactive remote access (IRA) through the jump host for approved firmware updates and configuration changes are covered by the vendor risk management programme.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `nerc_cip_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved outage tickets, audit period dates, relay test schedule entries, and vendor IRA session IDs. The search filters against this lookup and suppresses events where `cip_exception_approved=true` and the window is active.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `wineventlog` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-007-6 R5 Part 5.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.34: Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `wineventlog` under `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=wineventlog earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure 4625 auditing enabled. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Add threshold alerts for failures. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Correlate with password spray use cases. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Retain per entity log policy. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets," the aggregation summarises access activity — the control is about WHO accessed WHAT, so the key fields in the aggregate map directly to 'principal' and 'resource' for the compliance narrative.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` — the BES Cyber System inventory required for NERC CIP asset identification. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`bes_system`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `ComputerName`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval ComputerName = lower(ComputerName)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.13.34: Failed and Successful Login Attempt Monitoring on BES Assets]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.13.34,reg=NERC_CIP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `Account_Name`, `ComputerName`, `bes_system`, `outcome`. Run:

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table Account_Name, ComputerName, bes_system, outcome
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.13.34: NERC CIP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar (`success vs fail`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`top accounts`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`failure rate`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'NERC CIP current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NERC CIP-specific failure modes:**

• BES Cyber System classification (CIP-002): every NERC CIP detection must filter to BES Cyber Systems only. A general 'Windows login' search across the entire estate is not CIP evidence; a search filtered through `bes_cyber_asset_inventory.csv` is.

• Low-Impact vs Medium-Impact vs High-Impact: CIP requirements scale with impact rating. A detection that fires identically for Low and High-Impact systems over-applies controls (wastes IR effort) and under-applies audit attention. Include impact rating in the alert payload and route by rating.

• ERC (Electronic Security Perimeter) boundary: CIP-005 requires strict ESP documentation. Firewall-rule detections must reference the ESP boundary register — a rule change on a firewall that's not an EAP is NOT a CIP finding; a rule change on an EAP is.

• CIP-010 configuration baselines: baseline monitoring must use an approved baseline, not an informal one. Reference the CIP-010 attestation register in the UC; baselines without dated attestation signatures are not regulatory evidence.
grandmaExplanation: We summarize authentication successes and failures on BES Cyber Assets to support account lockout and misuse investigations. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
